execution_summary: I generated the Dockerfile, built the image, and started the container. The orchestrator executed a Python entrypoint (main.py -> replication_data/k17_data_prep__py.py -> replication_data/k17_analysis__py.py). The data prep step skipped regenerating the processed file because data/original/26/input/replication_data/k17_processed_data.csv was already present. The analysis script fitted hierarchical OLS models (Model 1 demographics, Model 2 + baseline T1 joviality, Model 3 focal model with REQ predictors and hassles) and an unstandardized Model 3a. Overall the pipeline executed successfully and followed the preregistered plan: standardized and unstandardized regressions were run, R-squared and incremental R2 reported, and exploratory outcomes were computed. No major deviations from the planned analysis were made, aside from small edits to set the Python entrypoint so the orchestrator could execute the Python scripts.

code_executed.command: orchestrator_execute_entry (ran main.py which invoked replication_data/k17_data_prep__py.py and replication_data/k17_analysis__py.py)
code_executed.logs: Processed data already available at: /workspace/replication_data/k17_processed_data.csv. Skipping data prep and proceeding to analysis. Analysis executed; Model 3 (standardized) reported req_relax_z coef = 0.0982 (SE = 0.057), t = 1.727, two-tailed p = 0.085, 95% CI = [-0.014, 0.210]. R-squared for Model 3 = 0.393; Incremental R2 (M3 - M2) = 0.0516. Sample used: 337 observations.
code_executed.environment: Python 3.10-slim Docker image with pandas>=1.4, numpy>=1.22, statsmodels>=0.13, scipy>=1.7 (per generated Dockerfile)

code_executed.command: python /workspace/replication_data/k17_analysis__py.py (executed inside container by the orchestration chain)
code_executed.logs: Multiple OLS regressions printed: Model 1 (R2 = 0.005), Model 2 (R2 = 0.341), Model 3 standardized (R2 = 0.393), Model 3a uncentered/raw (R2 = 0.393). F-tests comparing M1->M2 and M2->M3 printed; exploratory models for other affect outcomes printed. No runtime errors. Some large condition numbers noted in output suggesting possible multicollinearity warnings for certain outcomes.
code_executed.environment: Same Python 3.10 runtime and package set as above

results.hypothesis_tested: Focal preregistered hypothesis: Weekend relaxation (req_relax at T2) is positively associated with joviality at T3 (T3_panas_joviality), controlling for baseline T1 joviality and demographics/work controls.

findings_summary.statistical_significance: Two-tailed p = 0.085 (not significant at two-tailed alpha=0.05). One-tailed p ≈ 0.0425 (directional test); this meets a one-tailed alpha=0.05 per the preregistered directional hypothesis.
findings_summary.effect_size: Model 3 R-squared = 0.393. Incremental R2 when adding REQ predictors (M3 - M2) = 0.0516

findings_summary.outcome_name: Unstandardized coefficient for req_relax (Model 3a, raw-scale T3_panas_joviality)
findings_summary.effect_size: Model 3a R-squared = 0.393 (same variance explained as standardized model); sample size = 337

tables.table_description: OLS regression output for Model 3 (T3_panas_joviality_z regressed on demographics, T1 baseline, and REQ predictors incl. req_relax_z). Includes coefficients, SEs, t-stats, p-values, and 95% CIs.
